description: Behavior cases for staged schema evolution: expand/migrate/contract discipline, consumer enumeration beyond app code, live-rename decomposition, and the one-deploy-breaking-change refusal.

cases.prompt: We need to split the single `full_name` column on our users table into `given_name` and `family_name` without downtime — the app deploys rolling, and the warehouse syncs this table nightly.

assertions: Produces the three-stage plan: expand adds the two new nullable columns; migrate dual-writes and backfills with a parity gate; contract stops dual-writes, registers `full_name` in the deprecation register, and schedules (not executes) the drop behind zero-reader evidence.
States the compatibility guarantee per stage in both directions (old code x new schema, new code x old schema) for the rolling-deploy overlap.
Enumerates consumers beyond app code — explicitly dispositions the nightly warehouse sync as a reader that must move before contract.
Hands the backfill's operational execution to data-migration-runbook-author and routes each stage's migration file to secure-migration-reviewer rather than embedding either.

cases.prompt: Plan the retirement of our legacy `status` integer column in favor of the new `state` enum over the next three releases — old clients still write the integer today.

assertions: Maps stages to release boundaries: which release carries expand, which carries the read-switch, which is allowed to drop.
Requires dual-write while any deployed writer still produces the integer form, with a stated end condition rather than an open-ended window.
Creates a deprecation-register entry with replaced-by, earliest-drop release, required evidence, and an owner.
States rollback per stage and identifies the physical drop as the point of no return.

cases.prompt: Our migration tool supports RENAME COLUMN in one line — can we just rename `org_id` to `tenant_id` on the live table in tonight's deploy?

assertions: Explains that an in-place rename breaks whichever side deploys second during the rolling overlap — the one-line tooling support does not change the distributed-systems problem.
Decomposes the rename into add-new / dual-write / switch-reads / drop-old stages with gates.
Checks for non-app consumers of the old column name (events, analytics, raw queries) before any contract stage.
Does not simply refuse: presents the staged alternative and notes that accepting downtime instead is a human choice to be recorded.

cases.prompt: Just plan the DROP of the old columns first so the schema is clean, and we'll fix whatever code still reads them as errors come in.

assertions: Refuses destructive-first sequencing: a drop is only ever the final stage behind a zero-reader gate, because data loss cannot be staged around after the fact.
Re-sequences the request into expand/migrate/contract order instead of merely declining.
Cites the missing evidence (no zero-reader proof) as the blocking condition, not stylistic preference.
Does not produce any plan variant in which the drop precedes consumer disposition.

cases.prompt: Here's the migration file adding the new orders table with its grants and RLS policy — review it for safety before it ships.

assertions: Does not invoke schema-evolution-planner: a written migration needing privilege/RLS/lock/destructive-risk review is secure-migration-reviewer's job.
No evolution sequencing is requested — the change is a single additive migration already authored.
